revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Chōkaisan-Ōmonoimi-jinja]][[en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]][[es:Santuario Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi]][[fr:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi-jinja]][[ja:鳥海山大物忌神社]][[simple:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]][[zh:鳥海山大物忌神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine on mount Chokai|qq=}} {{Distinguish|Chōkai gassan ryōsho-gu|qq=}}{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | native_name = 鳥海山大物忌神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Sanchou-Gohonsha Shinden.JPG | caption = Altar of the shrine at the peak | deity = [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]], [[Toyoukebime]], and [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] }} '''Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|qq=}}), also known as Chokaisan Ōmonoimi-jinja, is a [[Shinto shrine]] on [[Mount Chokai]] in [[Yamagata Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Kōdansha |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WvApAQAAMAAJ&q=%C5%8Cmonoimi |title=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan |date=1983 |publisher=Kodansha |isbn=978-0-87011-620-9 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite web |date=2017-11-20 |title=Climbing a less-crowded mountain in Japan |url=https://okinawa.stripes.com/travel/climbing-less-crowded-mountain-japan |access-date=2023-10-26 |website=Stripes Okinawa |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=g2dRAAAAYAAJ&q=%C5%8Cmonoimi |title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books I-V |date=1970 |publisher=Sophia University |language=en|qq=}} The shrine has three parts on different parts of the mountain: Fukura-kuchinomiya and Warabioka-kuchinomiya at the foot of the mountain and easier to access for visitors, and the main shrine {{endash|qq=}} known as Sanchō-Gohonsha {{endash|qq=}} on the mountain's summit.{{cite web |title=chokaizan omonoimi shrine（warabioka kuchi no miya） - shrine-heritager |url=https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |url-status=unfit |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231111061123/https://shrineheritager.com/en/chokaizan-omonoimi-shrine/ |archive-date=2023-11-11 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=shrineheritager.com|qq=}}{{cite web |date=6 June 2022 |title=Chokaizan Omono-Imi Jinja - Must-See, Access, Hours & Price |url=https://www.gltjp.com/en/directory/item/12890/ |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=GOOD LUCK TRIP |language=en|qq=}} The Shrine is a national historic site. The shrine is primarily dedicated to [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]], the unique [[kami]] of the shrine, but also worships [[Toyoukebime]] and [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]].{{cite web |url=https://okunomichi.wordpress.com/2014/08/28/1301/ |title=Okunomichi |date=28 August 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141014191526/https://okunomichi.wordpress.com/2014/08/28/1301/ |archive-date=2014-10-14 |access-date=2023-11-04|qq=}}{{cite book |last=Fukada |first=Kyūya |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4VgEEAAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PT98 |title=One Hundred Mountains of Japan |date=2014-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-4785-2 |pages=76 |language=en|qq=}} Omonoimi no Kami is considered possibly identical to {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime|1=Toyouke-hime}}{{efn|Ōmonoimi-no-kami is believed to be the god of Mount Chōkai, or the northernmost post of the land of [[Yamato period|Yamato]].|qq=}}{{cite book |author1=Sonoda, Minoru |title=Nihon no kamigami no jiten : Shinto saishi to yaoyorozu no kamigami |author2=Mogi, Sakae |publisher=[[:ja:学研ホールディングス|Gakken]] |year=1997 |isbn=9784056016291 |series=Books esoterica, 2.; New sight mook |pages=68, 69 |language=ja |oclc=42978057 |ref={{sfnref|Sonoda and Mogi|1997|qq=}} |author-link=:ja:薗田稔|qq=}} who is also worshipped at the Warabioka subshrine. Omonoimi no Kami is primarily associated with industrial growth and is also enshrined in other places in the [[Tōhoku region]], including {{ill|Chōkai Gassan Ryōsho-gū|simple|4=ja|5=鳥海月山両所宮|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkai gassan ryōsho-gu|qq=}}.{{cite web |title=Enshrined Gods |url=https://kawawajinja.com/en/gosaijin.html |access-date=2023-06-18 |website=kawawajinja.com|qq=}} The shrine directly worships the mountain as a [[Kannabi]]. This is a rare practice and the only major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] are {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} and [[Suwa-taisha]] and [[Kanasana Shrine]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195|qq=}}{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en|qq=}} The shrine is the {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}} [[Ichinomiya]], or the first Ranked shrine of the {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}}.{{cite web |last=Suzuki |first=Masataka |title=Dewasanzan Shinkō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8579 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto|qq=}}{{Cite web |title=Nationwide List of Ichinomiya |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01|qq=}} It is close to the [[Three Mountains of Dewa]] which are also prominent spots of [[Mountain worship|Mountain Worship]]. It is similar to [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] at [[Mount Fuji]] == History == The mountain has been worshipped since ancient times, and its god [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] has been identified with the mountain itself. Traditions vary about the founding of the main shrine with some saying during it was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|qid=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}} and others saying it was founded during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}. The repeated destruction of the main shrine means archaeological evidence is difficult to find. However the two shrines at the foot of the mountain are known to have been founded in 564 in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}. Early mentions of the shrine include the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]] where it is listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]], a very highly Ranked shrine and mentions in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] whjere an oracle foretold the Imperial Army's defeat in 878 and 879. In history every time the volcano erupted, the [[Shinkai (divine rank)|Rank]] of [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] was increased. This meant more lands were given to the shrine and it became wealthier and more powerful.{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: Shinkai |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_s.html |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}} Eruptions were interpreted as his anger.{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Yui |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ae8yAQAAQBAJ&dq=%C5%8Cmonoimi&pg=PA96 |title=Medicine Master Buddha: The Iconic Worship of Yakushi in Heian Japan |date=2011-12-19 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-22917-4 |language=en|qq=}} Once it was said that a corpse was improperly disposed of near the mountain. At that point a massive eruption happened and dark muddy water flowed down the rivers which were dammed by dead fish.{{Cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Yui |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dgo7JV9vNCQC&dq=Chokaisan+-+Omonoimi&pg=PA96 |title=Medicine Master Buddha: The Iconic Worship of Yakushi in Heian Japan |date=2011-12-23 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-19601-8 |language=en|qq=}} [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] is known to have received the Rank of Shōichii in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|qid=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}. The shrine has been a prominent place of [[Shugendō|shugendo]] since the Middle Ages.{{Cite book |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781350179424 |title=Defining Shugendō |date=2020 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-17939-4 |editor-last=Castiglioni |editor-first=Andrea |pages=57 |doi=10.5040/9781350179424 |editor-last2=Rambelli |editor-first2=Fabio |editor-last3=Roth |editor-first3=Carina|qq=}} A medieval buddhist myth of the temple says it was founded by the Buddhist monk {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} for "Chokai Daigongen".{{Cite web |title=Chokai - Christopher E. Brennen |url=http://brennen.caltech.edu/world/chokai.htm |access-date=2023-11-11 |website=brennen.caltech.edu|qq=}} This myth is most associated with Warabioka. Other myths involve other Buddhist monks like {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|qid=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}} or [[Kūkai]]. The shrine was subject to a legal battle after [[World War II]] because people were confused about who owned the summit with the end of [[State Shinto]], as of 1966 the battle was still ongoing.{{cite journal |date=1966 |title=Reminiscences of Religion in Postwar Japan: Economic Changes after the War (Continued) |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30232985 |journal=Contemporary Religions in Japan |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=51–79 |issn=0010-7557 |jstor=30232985|qq=}} In 2008, the area from the summit to Kuchinomiya became a National Historic Site. == Festivals == The shrine has a festival every year on July 14 called Hi-awase shinji (火合せ神事). During the festival all across [[Yamagata Prefecture]] fires are lit simultaneously at this shrine and other [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi]] shrines including one in [[Sakata, Yamagata]] for good harvest and good fishing.{{Cite web |last=Mogi |first=Sakae |title=Hi-awase shinji |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9228 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230425181001/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9228 |archive-date=2023-04-25 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto|qq=}} == Sanchō-Gohonsha == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sanchō-Gohonsha | native_name = 山頂御本社 | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Precincts of Sanchou-Gohonsha.JPG | caption = Sanchō-Gohonsha | coordinates = {{Coord|39|5|51.43|N|140|2|55.21|E|region:JP-06_scale:10000|name=山頂御本社|qq=}} | deity = [[Ōmonoimi no Kami|Omonoimi no Kami]] }} Sanchō-Gohonsha is the main shrine and it is located on the peak of [[Mount Chokai]]. It looks like an ordinary cabin on the outside.https://archive.today/20230425185131/https://akitajet.com/wiki/Mount_Ch%C5%8Dkai The shrine is rebuilt once every 20 years, it was rebuilt in {{ill|1997|en|1997|qid=Q2088|lt=1997|1=1997}}. ===Gallery=== File:鳥海山 大物忌引神社2010.08.13.JPG|Very distant photo demonstrating the scale of the mountain File:Chokaisan Omonoimi Jinja Sancho Honden.jpg|Birds eye view of the shrine complex File:Sanchou-Gohonsha Gate.JPG|Torii File:大物忌神社と御室.jpg|Torii from behind File:Precincts of Sanchou-Gohonsha.JPG|Approaching the Honden File:Sanchou-Gohonsha Shinden.JPG|Mountain-top Main Shrine Honden {{clear|qq=}} == Fukura-kuchinomiya == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Fukura-kuchinomiya | native_name = 吹浦口之宮 | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Hukura haiden.JPG | caption = Fukura-kuchinomiya | coordinates = {{Coord|39|04|30.45|N|139|52|44.05|E|region:JP-06_scale:10000|name=吹浦口之宮|qq=}} | deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] }} Fukura-kuchinomiya is a subshrine located at the foot of the mountain. It is located 15 km west of the main shrine and 10 km northwest of Warabioka-kuchinomiya Local tradition says the shrine was founded by {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|qid=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}} The [[Honden]] is located on a hill and faces south. [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] is enshrined here. A lake called Maruikesama is worshipped nearby as a [[Kannabi]]. The lake is considered to be a Kami itself rather than merely one living in it.{{Efn|Lake coordinates: {{Coord|39|4|22|N|139|53|30|E|region:JP-06_scale:10000|name=吹浦口之宮|qq=}} }}{{cite web |title=Maruikesama |url=https://www.pref.yamagata.jp/documents/2216/maruikesama-e.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220502133106/https://www.pref.yamagata.jp/documents/2216/maruikesama-e.pdf |archive-date=2022-05-02 |access-date=2023-11-04|qq=}} ===Gallery=== File:Hukura-kuchinomiya Gate.JPG|Entrance Torii File:Hukura haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Hukura Ryoushogu.JPG|Honden File:Hukura Honden.JPG|Honden from different angle {{clear|qq=}} == Warabioka-kuchinomiya == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Warabioka-kuchinomiya | native_name = 蕨岡口之宮 | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = 蕨口之宮 一の鳥居.jpg | caption = Warabioka-kuchinomiya | coordinates = {{Coord|38|59|49.00|N|139|56|38.00|E|region:JP-06_scale:10000|name=蕨岡口之宮|qq=}} | deity = [[Toyoukebime]],
[[Ukanomitama]]| }} Warabioka-kuchinomiya is a subshrine located at the foot of the mountain. It is located 10 km southeast of Fukura-kuchinomiya, and 14 km southwest of Sancho-Gohonsha. The shrine worships agricultural deities [[Toyoukebime|Toyoke-Ookami]] and [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]. It is said in local tradition that the legendary Buddhist monk {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} founded the shrine. The shrine has four small [[Hokora]] or subshrines of its own. There is a festival celebrated here on May 3 called Daimonbei Matsuri. There are three [[torii]] gates and the movie [[Rurouni Kenshin: The Legend Ends]] was filmed there.{{Cite web |title=Warabioka Kuchinomiya Shrine |url=https://www.guidoor.jp/en/spots/207 |access-date=2023-04-25 |website=Guidoor |language=en|qq=}} ===Gallery=== File:蕨口之宮 一の鳥居.jpg|alt=Located at the base of the sanctuary.|First Torii File:Warabioka-kuchinomiya Gate.JPG|Second Torii File:Warabioka-kuchinomiya 2nd Gate.JPG|Third Torii File:Warabioka Honden.JPG|Honden {{clear|qq=}} ==See also== * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] * {{ill|Chōkai Gassan Ryōsho-gū|ja|鳥海月山両所宮|simple|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkai gassan ryōsho-gu|qq=}} * [[Three Mountains of Dewa]] ** [[Mount Gassan]] with [[Gassan Shrine]] ** [[Mount Haguro]] with {{ill|Dewa Shrine|en|Dewa Shrine|qid=Q11395802|lt=Hagurosan Shrine|1=Dewa Shrine}} ** [[Mount Yudono]] with {{ill|Yudonosan Shrine|en|Yudonosan Shrine|qid=Q11563607|lt=Yudonosan Shrine|1=Yudonosan Shrine}} * Other highly Ranked shrines of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}} ** Ninomiya {{ill|Kinowa Shrine|ja|城輪神社|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinowa Shrine|qq=}} ** Sannomiya {{ill|Omoimi Shrine|ja|小物忌神社|1=Omonoimi Shrine (Yamadate, Sakata)|2=ja|3=小物忌神社|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omoimi Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Omonoimi Shrine (Yamadate, Sakata)|qq=}} ==Notelist== {{notelist|qq=}} ==References== {{reflist|qq=}} {{Omonoimi Faith|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|version=97314356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] [[ja:鳥海山大物忌神社]] {{Commons category|Chōkaisan-Ōmonoimi-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi]] [[fr:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi-jinja]] [[simple:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] [[zh:鳥海山大物忌神社]] {{Commons category|Chōkaisan-Ōmonoimi-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi]] [[fr:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi-jinja]] [[simple:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]] [[zh:鳥海山大物忌神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11173679}} [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Third Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Dewa Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Dewa Province]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:山形県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:山形県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:山形県の重要文化財]]
